request.question: Walter W. Arndt translated many poems by the poet and novelist of what dual nationality?

title: Burton Raffel
Xie Zhen (poet)
Rainer Maria Rilke
Multiple citizenship
Banalata Sen (book)
Milorad Pavić
Sino-Indonesian Dual Nationality Treaty
Walter W. Arndt
Madah Kelana
Mexican immigration to Spain
sentences: Burton Nathan Raffel (April 27, 1928 – September 29, 2015) was a translator, a poet and a teacher.
He translated many poems, including the Anglo-Saxon epic "Beowulf", poems by Horace, and "Gargantua and Pantagruel" by François Rabelais.
In 1964, Raffel recorded an album along with Robert P. Creed, on Folkways Records entitled "Lyrics from the Old English."
In 1996, he published his translation of Miguel de Cervantes' "Don Quixote", which has been acclaimed for making Cervantes more accessible to the modern generation.
In 2006, Yale University Press published his new translation of the Nibelungenlied.
Xie Zhen (谢榛, 1495－1575) was a Chinese poet of the Ming Dynasty.
When he was 15 years old, he learned to write poems after Su Donggao (苏东皋).
At age 16, some "yuefu" poems he composed became very popular in Linqing, Deping and nearby.
Most of his poems depicted the landscape of Yecheng, where he lived for many years.
In 1548, Xia Yan and Zeng Xian were executed in a political struggle against Yan Song, and some of Xie's friends were banished from the court in the aftermath, Xie wrote many poems to console them.

René Karl Wilhelm Johann Josef Maria Rilke (4 December 1875 – 29 December 1926)—better known as Rainer Maria Rilke (] )—was a Bohemian-Austrian poet and novelist, "widely recognized as one of the most lyrically intense German-language poets", writing in both verse and highly lyrical prose.
Several critics have described Rilke's work as inherently "mystical".

His writings include one novel, several collections of poetry, and several volumes of correspondence in which he invokes haunting images that focus on the difficulty of communion with the ineffable in an age of disbelief, solitude, and profound anxiety.
These deeply existential themes tend to position him as a transitional figure between the traditional and the modernist writers.

Multiple citizenship, also called dual citizenship or multiple nationality or dual nationality, is a person's citizenship status, in which a person is concurrently regarded as a citizen of more than one state under the laws of those states.
There is no international convention which determines the nationality or citizen status of a person, which is defined exclusively by national laws, which vary and can be inconsistent with each other.
Multiple citizenship arises because different countries use different, and not necessarily mutually exclusive, criteria for citizenship.
Colloquial speech refers to people "holding" multiple citizenship but technically each nation makes a claim that this person be considered its national.
Banalata Sen (Bengali: বনলতা সেন ) is a volume of poems by Jibanananda Das.

During Das's lifetime, it was published twice: first time in Poush 1349 Bengali calendar(December 1942 AD) with a cover by Sambhu Shaha including 12 poems, second time in Srabon 1359 Bengali calendar (1952 AD) an enlarged version with a cover by Satyajit Ray including 30 poems.
Das named the volume after the poem: Banalata Sen, one of Das’s finest poems, certainly his most popular.
The enlarged edition published by Signet Press was awarded in 1953 at the Nikhil Banga Rabindra Sahitya Sammelan (All Bengal Rabindra Literature Convention).
The recurring themes in the poems of this volume are love, nature, time, temporariness of life and love, etc.
Above all, a historical sense pervades everything.
The names that frequent in many poems are Suchetana, Suranjana, Sudarshana and Syamali and these women are deemed above or beyond women in general.
In these poems, the love Das talks about crosses the boundaries of time and place and sometimes seems impersonal too.
Milorad Pavić (Serbian Cyrillic: , ] ; 15 October 1929 – 30 November 2009) was a Serbian novelist, poet, short story writer, and literary historian.
Born in Belgrade in 1929, he published many poems, short stories and novels during his lifetime, the most famous of which was the "Dictionary of the Khazars" (1984).
Upon its release, it was hailed as "the first novel of the 21st century."
Pavić's works have been translated into more than thirty languages.
He was vastly popular in Europe and in South America, and was deemed "one of the most intriguing writers from the beginning of the 21st century."
He won numerous prizes in Serbia and in the former Yugoslavia, and was mentioned several times as a potential candidate for the Nobel Prize in Literature.
He died in Belgrade in 2009.
The Sino-Indonesian Dual Nationality Treaty was a bilateral agreement between the People's Republic of China and the Republic of Indonesia on the issue of the dual nationality of Chinese Indonesians.
It was signed by Zhou Enlai, Premier and Foreign Minister of China, and Sunario, Foreign Minister of Indonesia, on 22 April 1955 during the Asian–African Conference in Bandung.
Following ratification by both parties, the treaty came into force on 20 January 1960 after an exchange of the instruments of ratification in Beijing.
Walter Werner Arndt (May 4, 1916-February 15, 2011) was a world-renowned scholar and translator of Russian, German and Polish.
At the time of his death, he was the Sherman Fairchild Professor of Humanities, Emeritus, of Russian Language and Literature at Dartmouth College.

With degrees in Business Administration from Warsaw University, in Political Science and Economics from Oxford University (Oriel College), a Masters in Engineering from Robert College (Istanbul), and a PhD.

in Comparative Literature from UNC, Chapel Hill, Arndt was well known for his metric translations, which included versions of Goethe's "Faust", Aleksandr Pushkin's "Eugene Onegin", a number of poems by Rainer Maria Rilke, as well as works by Busch, Morgenstern, and others.
His translation of "Eugene Onegin" won the Bollingen Poetry Translation Prize in 1962.
Madah Kelana (or in English is Kelana hymn) is a collection of poems from a poet from Indonesia Poedjangga Baroe Era, Sanusi Pane.
This collection of poems was published in 1931 and is his second poem book.
These poems tells the story of a wanderer who seek happiness and finally found what he was looking for it in itself.
Sanusi Pane made this Madah Kelana after touring India since 1929 to 1930.
The characteristic of this poems are romantic and we can see many poems on the theme of love.
Mexican immigration to Spain refers to the Mexican population in Spain and their Spanish-born descendants.
The Mexicans living in Spain are composed primarily of students, skilled professionals, spouses of Spaniards, as well as Mexican citizens who also have Spanish nationality.

In December 2008, the National Statistics Institute in Spain had 14,399 registered Mexicans within its territory, of which 7,210 hold other nationalities of the European Union or are family members of EU citizens.
To this number must be added those with dual nationality, who are not in Spanish records as foreigners.
Mexican and Spanish laws allow dual citizenship, and many Mexicans who have asked for it, whether they are residents in Spain as grandchildren or they are children of Spanish migrants to Mexico.

In 2010, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Mexico recorded 21,107 Mexicans living in Spain, who became the third largest Mexican community residing abroad, after the United States and Canada; and the largest Mexican community in Europe.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the single field `summary`.

Goal: compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a short intermediate state that makes it easy for the next-hop agent to (a) see what facts are present per candidate/entity, (b) see what is ambiguous or missing, and (c) know 1–4 prioritized next-hop retrieval targets or concise queries. This module does not produce the final answer unless the provided passages already unambiguously contain every fact required to resolve the question's relation for every argument.

Procedure:
1) Decide whether the question's relation is semantically complete given the supplied passages. Treat the hop as INCOMPLETE if any of the following hold: a) the relation requires combining or comparing facts across pages (e.g., comparing birth dates, publication/founding years, ordinal ranks, confirming a named director among candidates) and the passages do not supply each required fact unambiguously for all relevant arguments; b) any relation argument is ambiguous or has multiple candidate pages or mentions in the passages; c) an expected candidate page or explicit fact for an argument is missing (e.g., only one side of a comparison is present). Do not trigger INCOMPLETE solely from surface tokens (years, digits) — base the decision on whether the relation's arguments are unambiguously resolved.

2) If the hop is COMPLETE (all required facts to resolve the relation for every argument are present unambiguously in the passages): produce a concise derived result in `summary`. This may be a short phrase or sentence that states the necessary fact(s) needed by the pipeline. Keep it minimal and grounded in the passages; you may include a 1–2 token citation (passage title) if helpful.

3) If the hop is INCOMPLETE or AMBIGUOUS: do NOT assert a final answer or perform the final comparison/selection. Instead emit a short structured report in `summary` containing these labeled elements (prefer bullets or a compact paragraph with explicit labels). Keep all text neutral and non-assertive.

  - evidence: For each named candidate/argument mentioned in the question or passages, list the candidate label and the exact passage title(s) that mention it plus a very short quoted snippet or explicit fact token found there (1–12 words). If a candidate is not mentioned, say so briefly.

  - ambiguities_or_missing: Explicitly name any ambiguities (e.g., multiple candidates for the same name, multiple pages that could be the intended entity) and any missing expected pages or facts.

  - missing_facts_or_bridge: State exactly which fact(s) or disambiguation is needed to complete the relation (e.g., "birth date for Person A", "publication year of Work B", "director credit for Film C"). Be as specific and minimal as possible.

  - retrieval_direction: Provide 1–4 prioritized next-hop targets or concise queries to resolve the missing facts/ambiguities. Prefer explicit page titles when available (exact if known), otherwise provide short search-style queries (few words) or a clear description of the document to retrieve. Order targets by priority.

Formatting and style constraints (apply to both COMPLETE and INCOMPLETE cases):
- The output must be a single `summary` field only. Do not emit other fields.
- In INCOMPLETE reports, use neutral, non-assertive phrasing. Do not produce final comparisons, numeric answers, or definitive selections (e.g., avoid "X is the oldest" or "Answer: 3").
- Keep the report compact and actionable (short labeled bullets or one concise paragraph). Avoid long prose.
- It is allowed and encouraged to quote short factual snippets from passages and cite passage titles to show grounding.
- Do not defer or mark INCOMPLETE when the passages already unambiguously provide all facts for the relation and its arguments.

Produce the `summary` content that follows these rules.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the single field `query`.

Goal
- Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-intent query suitable for a second-hop search that will recover the missing bridging evidence needed to answer the question. Do not produce the final answer in `query`.

Semantic decision rule (when to emit a retrieval-intent query)
- If summary_1 does NOT assert a confirmed linking entity or verified bridging fact needed to answer the question, you MUST emit exactly one compact retrieval-intent query. "Does not assert a confirmed bridge" includes short or candidate-only summaries (e.g., a single named entity, a quoted candidate title, or numeric anchors that are not verified) and any case where the visible anchors do not unambiguously identify the needed bridge.
- If summary_1 already names a confirmed bridge (a clearly identified, verified linking entity or unambiguous metadata that directly connects the question's pieces), you may produce a compact query to fetch supporting evidence, but do NOT produce an answer in the `query` field.
- If summary_1 explicitly states that the bridge cannot be recovered automatically (extreme ambiguity or user-local context requiring clarification), do NOT force a misleading retrieval query. In that case, set `query` to the single short token BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE (all caps, no punctuation) so downstream steps can prompt the user for clarification. Do not invent facts.

Query construction rules
- Output exactly one compact line of text as `query`. Keep it short and retrieval-focused (few keywords, no full sentences). Use visible anchors from `question` and `summary_1` and minimal retrieval keywords that express the missing relation (e.g., anchor1 anchor2 which artist recorded both; AlbumTitle album artist genre; 1991–92 NHL season regular season).
- Do not include the final answer, assertions, or user-facing instructions in the `query` field. Avoid polite phrasing, questions to the user, or explanatory text. Prefer noun phrases and relation keywords.
- If your natural impulse would be to write a declarative answer or a clarification sentence in `query`, instead convert that output into a compact retrieval-intent query that targets the missing bridge.
- Preserve semantic fidelity: base the query only on visible anchors and relations in `question` and `summary_1`. Do not invent hidden identifiers or assume unseen facts.

Format contract
- Return exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short string. If unrecoverable as described above, set `query` to BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Examples of disallowed outputs for `query`
- A declarative answer (e.g., "The artist was X.")
- A user-facing clarification sentence (e.g., "Do you mean X or Y?") unless summary_1 explicitly requires clarification — in that case use BRIDGE_UNRECOVERABLE.

Behavioral constraints
- Do not use surface heuristics (token count, punctuation, or quotation marks) as the trigger; apply the semantic rule above.
- Emit only one compact retrieval string when the bridge is missing; do not emit multiple queries or long explanations.

Inputs available: `question`, `summary_1`. Output required: single field `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`. The `summary` must be a concise, post-retrieval integration of the evidence that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Follow these rules when composing `summary`:

- Purpose: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` into a grounded state that retains or resolves the earlier bridge only when the second-hop passages supply relation- or attribute-facts that are necessary to answer or disambiguate the `question` (for example: partnership/membership links, joint-award partners indicated by 'with', or requested biographical qualifiers such as nationality, birthdate, current team/status).

- When to append supporting sentence(s): Only append short passage-grounded supporting sentence(s) when the question's semantic intent requires relation or attribute facts to answer or disambiguate. In those cases, produce a compact integrated summary that (a) preserves the concise primary answer or canonical label/token chosen from `context`, and (b) appends one short supporting sentence per relevant entity (or one joint sentence for explicit partnerships) that is directly supported by the supplied `passages` and identity-aligned to the entity referenced in `context`.

- When not to append: Do not append supporting sentences and instead return only the canonical label/token when any of the following apply: the question expects a normalized identifier or single token (examples: binary/multiple-choice label selection between named options, identification-only questions, or explicit token-extraction such as 'middle name'); or the `context` already contains a full, passage-consistent, multi-clause integrated answer. These are semantic exceptions — do not append merely because an answer in `context` is short.

- Identity alignment and verification: Before appending, confirm that the fact in `passages` refers to the same entity referenced in `context` (match names, common aliases, roles, dates, or other corroborating attributes). If identity is ambiguous or unsupported, refrain from appending and preserve uncertainty instead.

- Style and content constraints: Keep output concise. If appending, limit `summary` to 1–3 sentences total (the primary concise answer plus at most one short supporting sentence per relevant entity or one joint supporting sentence). Avoid inventing facts, avoid long verbatim quotations from `passages`, and avoid name-collision errors. Do not change which label or name is selected by the existing answer-selection logic.

- If there is insufficient information in `context` and `passages` to resolve the needed relation/attribute, summarize the uncertainty succinctly rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Produce only the `summary` field as the integrated output string.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use only summary_1 and summary_2 as evidence. Treat summary_2 as the later, integrated evidence and prioritize it when reconciling information; use summary_1 only as the earlier, pre-second-hop context. Do not assume the final state is unresolved solely because summary_1 contains bridging or retrieval-oriented language.

Produce exactly one field, answer, whose value is the minimal final answer string that is fully supported by the visible summaries. The answer field must contain only that minimal plain-text string and nothing else (no explanation, no surrounding quotes, no JSON wrappers, no debugging or transport artifacts).

Decide whether the question's intent is binary (a truth-value / yes-no question) or non-binary (asking for a name, title, date, place, numeric count, selection, or other factual value). Make this decision semantically (based on the question's meaning), not by surface heuristics like token counts or punctuation.

- If the question's intent is binary AND both summary_1 and summary_2 unambiguously agree on the same truth-value, emit exactly one lowercase token: either yes or no (and nothing else) as the answer value.

- For all other question intents (non-binary), emit the minimal factual string supported by the summaries exactly as needed: preserve multi-token values, necessary punctuation, full date formats, and place formats (for example, full names, "City, State", or "Month Day, Year"), and do not truncate these to a single token.

If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not jointly support an unambiguous minimal answer, do not fabricate or over-assert; follow the system's established fallback behavior for ambiguous or unsupported questions.

Always forbid emitting serialized queries, JSON/dict-like structures, internal-debug wrappers, or other transport artifacts as the final answer. The answer must be plain text only.

Make all judgments about minimality and agreement based on the semantic content of summary_1 and summary_2, with priority to summary_2 when they differ.

Produce the answer value exactly as specified above in the answer field.

response.hop1_query: Walter W. Arndt translated many poems by the poet and novelist of what dual nationality?
hop1_docs: The Panther (poem) | The Panther (subtitled: "In Jardin des Plantes, Paris") is a poem by Rainer Maria Rilke written on 6 November 1902.  It describes a captured panther behind bars, as it was exhibited in the Ménagerie of the Jardin des Plantes in Paris.  It is one of Rilke's most famous poems and has been translated into English many times, including by many distinguished translators of Rilke, like Stephen Mitchell, C. F. MacIntyre, J. B. Leishman and Walter Arndt,Jessie Lamont and poets like Robert Bly.

Walter W. Arndt | Walter Werner Arndt (May 4, 1916-February 15, 2011) was a world-renowned scholar and translator of Russian, German and Polish.  At the time of his death, he was the Sherman Fairchild Professor of Humanities, Emeritus, of Russian Language and Literature at Dartmouth College.  With degrees in Business Administration from Warsaw University, in Political Science and Economics from Oxford University (Oriel College), a Masters in Engineering from Robert College (Istanbul), and a PhD.  in Comparative Literature from UNC, Chapel Hill, Arndt was well known for his metric translations, which included versions of Goethe's "Faust", Aleksandr Pushkin's "Eugene Onegin", a number of poems by Rainer Maria Rilke, as well as works by Busch, Morgenstern, and others.  His translation of "Eugene Onegin" won the Bollingen Poetry Translation Prize in 1962.

T. Vasudeva Reddy | T. Vasudeva Reddy (born.21 Dec.1943), a retired Principal and formerly U.G.C (University Grants Commission) National Fellow and Visiting Professor in English from Tirupati, Andhra Pradesh, is a poet, novelist and critic in English.  His poems have appeared in numerous journals and anthologies in India and abroad.  He has authored twelve collections of poems, two novels and three critical works.  His poems have appeared in French journals in Paris and in a recent anthology in Canada.  Many critical articles and research dissertations have appeared on his poetry.  He is now Hon.President of GIEWEC (Guild of Indian English Writers Editors and Critics).  His poems are translated in many languages.

Roy Croft | Roy Croft is a poet frequently given credit for writing a poem titled "Love" and beginning "I love you not only for what you are, but for what I am when I am with you." .  The poem, which is commonly used in wedding speeches and readings is quoted frequently.  It was included in a 1936 anthology entitled "Best Loved Poems of American People", edited by a Hazel Felleman, and published by Doubleday (ISBN  ) and appears without further attribution in "The Family Book of Best Loved Poems", edited by David L. George and published in 1952 by Doubleday & Company, Inc., then of Garden City, New York.  German translations of the poem circulate with the title "Ich liebe Dich" ("I Love You") but are (wrongly) credited to the Austrian poet Erich Fried.  The translation(s) led to the speculation that the poem was just a translation of Erich Fried's work and Roy Croft was a pseudonym used by a translator who wanted to keep all royalties from publication (rather than sharing them with Fried's estate) or who simply did not want to go through the trouble of obtaining a license from a foreign entity.  Taking into account that the poem was already published in 1936 (where Erich Fried was only 15) it seems very unlikely that Erich Fried could be the author.

Prafull Shiledar | Prafull Shiledar (born 1962) is an Indian Marathi language poet resides in Mumbai in India.  He holds degrees in Sciences, Marathi literature and English Literature.  He writes poetry in Marathi – a language in Central India spoken by about 120 million people.  His published poetry books are Swagat (A Monologue) (1993) and Jaganyachya Pasaryat (In the disarray of living) (2006) and many uncollected poems.  His poems are translated in many Indian languages and in English.  His poems are published in National and International journals and anthologies.  He has read poetry in many National literature festivals.

Joseph Auslander | Joseph Auslander (October 11, 1897 – June 22, 1965) was an American poet, anthologist, translator of poems, and novelist.  Auslander was appointed the first Poet Laureate Consultant in Poetry to the Library of Congress from 1937 and 1941.

Piotr Kochanowski | Piotr Kochanowski (1566-1620) was a Polish nobleman, poet and translator.  He belonged to a family of writers.  He was a son of Mikołaj Kochanowski and a nephew of Jan Kochanowski.  He was born in 1566 in Sycyna.  He is famous for his translations from Italian.  He translated into Polish what were generally esteemed to be the two greatest modern epic poems: Ludovico Ariosto's "Orlando furioso" ("Roland Enraged") and Torquato Tasso's "Gerusalemme liberata" ("Jerusalem Delivered").  His version of Tasso's poem served as the Polish national epic.  Piotr Kochanowski was the second poet in Poland (after Sebastian Grabowiecki) to use ottava rima, which became very popular in Baroque Polish poetry.  He died on 2 August 1620 and was buried at the Franciscan church in Cracow.  He is commonly regarded as one of the most important Polish writers of the Renaissance.
response.summary_1:
- evidence:
  - Rainer Maria Rilke — mentioned in "The Panther (poem)" as author ("a poem by Rainer Maria Rilke") and that it "has been translated ... by ... Walter Arndt" (passage [1]).
  - Rainer Maria Rilke — listed in "Walter W. Arndt" as one he translated ("a number of poems by Rainer Maria Rilke") (passage [2]).
  - Other authors Arndt translated (listed in passage [2]): Goethe ("Faust"), Aleksandr Pushkin ("Eugene Onegin"), Busch, Morgenstern — quoted: "versions of Goethe's 'Faust', Aleksandr Pushkin's 'Eugene Onegin', a number of poems by Rainer Maria Rilke, as well as works by Busch, Morgenstern" (passage [2]).

- ambiguities_or_missing:
  - None of the passages state any nationality for Rainer Maria Rilke (or indicate a dual-nationality label).
  - It's unclear which translated author the question intends if not Rilke; multiple translated authors are listed.

- missing_facts_or_bridge:
  - The specific dual-nationality descriptor for Rainer Maria Rilke (e.g., "Austro‑Bohemian", "Bohemian‑Austrian", etc.), or confirmation which translated author is described as a "poet and novelist" with a dual nationality.

- retrieval_direction (priority order):
  1. "Rainer Maria Rilke" — main biographical page (verify nationality phrase and that he is described as poet and novelist).
  2. Search query: Rainer Maria Rilke nationality "Austro-Bohemian" or "Bohemian-Austrian" (if page wording varies).

response.hop2_query: Rainer Maria Rilke nationality Austro-Bohemian poet novelist
hop2_docs: Rainer Maria Rilke | René Karl Wilhelm Johann Josef Maria Rilke (4 December 1875 – 29 December 1926)—better known as Rainer Maria Rilke (] )—was a Bohemian-Austrian poet and novelist, "widely recognized as one of the most lyrically intense German-language poets", writing in both verse and highly lyrical prose.  Several critics have described Rilke's work as inherently "mystical".  His writings include one novel, several collections of poetry, and several volumes of correspondence in which he invokes haunting images that focus on the difficulty of communion with the ineffable in an age of disbelief, solitude, and profound anxiety.  These deeply existential themes tend to position him as a transitional figure between the traditional and the modernist writers.
Rilke (disambiguation) | The name Rilke is often associated with Rainer Maria Rilke (1875–1926), Bohemian-Austrian poet ("Duino Elegies", "Sonnets to Orpheus").

The Book of Images | The Book of Images (German: Das Buch der Bilder ) is a collection of poetry by the Bohemian-Austrian poet and novelist Rainer Maria Rilke (1875-1926).  It was first published in 1902 by Axel Juncker Verlag.

Letters to a Young Poet | Letters to a Young Poet (original title, in German: Briefe an einen jungen Dichter) is a collection of ten letters written by Bohemian-Austrian poet Rainer Maria Rilke (1875–1926) to Franz Xaver Kappus (1883–1966), a 19-year-old officer cadet at the Theresian Military Academy in Wiener Neustadt.  Rilke, the son of an Austrian army officer, had studied at the academy's lower school at Sankt Pölten in the 1890s.  Kappus corresponded with the popular poet and author from 1902 to 1908 seeking his advice as to the quality of his poetry, and in deciding between a literary career or a career as an officer in the Austro-Hungarian Army.  Kappus compiled and published the letters in 1929—three years after Rilke's death from leukemia.

Rainer Maria Rilke Foundation | The Rainer Maria Rilke Foundation (in French: "Fondation Rainer Maria Rilke") was established in 1986 in Sierre, Switzerland, on the patronage of the municipality.  Its goal is to promote the knowledge of Rainer Maria Rilke's works, through a museum, exhibitions, lectures, conferences, publications and a festival.  The famous poet spent the five last years of his life in the city, living in the Château de Muzot, a 13th-century fortified manor on the edge of town.

The Book of Hours | The Book of Hours (German: Das Stunden-Buch ) is a collection of poetry by the Bohemian-Austrian poet and novelist Rainer Maria Rilke (1875-1926).  The collection was written between 1899 and 1903 in three parts, and first published in Leipzig by Insel Verlag in April 1905.  With its dreamy, melodic expression and neo-Romantic mood, it stands, along with 'The Lay of the Love and Death of Christoph Cornet', as the most important of his early works.

New Poems | New Poems (German: Neue Gedichte ) is a two-part collection of poems written by Bohemian-Austrian poet and novelist Rainer Maria Rilke (1875–1926).  The first volume, dedicated to Elisabeth and Karl von der Heydt was composed from 1902 to 1907 and was published in the same year by Insel Verlag in Leipzig.  The second volume (New Poems: The Other Part), dedicated to Auguste Rodin, was completed in 1908 and published by the same publisher.  With the exception of eight poems written in Capri, Rilke composed most of them in Paris and Meudon.  At the start of each volume he placed, respectively, Früher Apollo (Early Apollo) and Archaïscher Torso Apollos (Archaic Torso of Apollo), poems about sculptures of the poet-God.
response.summary_2: Bohemian-Austrian. Rainer Maria Rilke is described in the cited sources as a Bohemian-Austrian poet and novelist.
